_attribution: LA FORJA team (Manuel Peña Zúñiga). Original item authored by the team.
stem: Two fair six-sided dice are rolled. It is known that at least one of them shows a 6. What is the probability that both show a 6?
author_rationale: The author marked 1/6, reasoning that 'the other die must come up 6, and that happens with probability 1/6'. This reasoning is INCORRECT: it ignores the conditioning on the event 'at least one 6', which does not fix which of the dice is the 6.

intended_defect.description: The marked key (1/6) is wrong. The conditioning event 'at least one 6' has 11 favorable outcomes out of 36; of those, only 1 has both dice showing 6.
intended_defect.expected_finding: The bounded solver must compute P(both 6 | at least one 6) = |{(6,6)}| / |{at least one 6}| = 1/11, contradicting the marked key.

source.excerpt: For events A and B with P(B) > 0, conditional probability is defined as P(A|B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B). In a finite equiprobable sample space this equals the ratio of the number of outcomes in A ∩ B to the number of outcomes in B.
source.relevance: Fixes the definition that determines the correct denominator (the 11 outcomes of the conditioning event), which is exactly the step the author skipped.
